figures.gloss: The supreme 'High God' and impersonal Heaven that grants and withdraws the mandate of kings.
The two interpenetrating principles — dark and light, receptive and creative — each holding the seed of the other; the root of the I Ching's sixty-four changes.
The Creative (pure yang, Heaven) and the Receptive (pure yin, Earth) — the first two trigrams and parents of the other six.
The nameless way that gives rise to the one, the two, and the ten-thousand things — the source you can never stand outside of.

edges.note: The one source behind the ten-thousand things, knowable only by ceasing to stand outside it.
The supreme principle as an irreducible male-female pair: Heaven/Earth, Lord/Lady.
William Douglas Horden's The Toltec I Ching joins the Mesoamerican tonal/nagual to the Chinese yin/yang — a real synthesis from a real lineage, the join this whole project is named for.

Two impersonal ground-of-reality concepts that the world and its order arise from and conform to — the Dreaming's everywhen-Law and the Dao — neither a personal creator, both the abiding pattern beneath things. The cosmic-order answer reached independently at opposite edges of the earth.
Both are supreme sky-powers worshipped less directly than the nearer gods/ancestors — high heaven held in awe at a distance.
GOLD SEAM: Both systems figure ultimate reality and perfection not as the triumph of one polarity, but as the total, seamless fusion of fundamental opposites.
GOLD SEAM: The formless, nameless source substance of the universe, existing everywhere yet impossible to pin down with rational classification.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The cosmos defined by the necessary convergence of opposed forces, though Tinku emphasizes violent, kinetic friction over fluid harmony.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Distant, supreme celestial patriarchs whose ancient worship was somewhat displaced by more immediate, systemic natural forces.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A supreme, distant celestial creator who oversees cosmic order but is accessed primarily through lower, specialized spirits and ancestors.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An impersonal, flowing principle of cosmic dualism and constant generation rather than a static creator god.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A distant, supreme celestial orchestrator who presides over a vast bureaucracy of lesser entities.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A supreme, somewhat distant sovereign deity whose primary role is unifying and overseeing a vast, active bureaucracy of lower spirits.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A universally applied philosophy of necessary, complementary opposites, visually manifested in everyday aesthetics (saput poleng vs taijitu).
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both posit a profound, unnamable ultimate reality that is fundamentally empty of fixed form, yet acts as the generative matrix for all conditioned existence.
Impersonal cosmic process unfolding by its own law without a willing creator — the kalpas and the Dao both put order in the place where other traditions put a god.
Both name an order with no personal maker behind it — cosmic-order as the floor of reality; a true functional convergence, distinct families.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Cao Đài doctrine explicitly identifies its Supreme Being as the Jade Emperor/Shangdi of the Chinese pantheon.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Holy Mother is formally defined as the absolute manifestation of the Yin principle in the cosmos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the fundamental, amoral engine of reality created by the perfect, spinning union of dualistic opposites.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Donghak deliberately used the traditional East Asian name for the Supreme Ruler (Sangje) to localize its distinctly immanent theology.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The deliberate unification of male and female principles within a single entity to achieve cosmic balance and spiritual power.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A state of ultimate, intuitive alignment where the rigid division between the observer and the observed reality completely dissolves.
GOLD SEAM: In early Zhou dynasty usage, the sky deity Tian was explicitly merged with the Shang dynasty's high god Shangdi, eventually becoming a more impersonal moral force.
GOLD SEAM: The supreme, inexorable flow of the cosmos, though Confucianism frames it in explicitly moral and socio-political terms rather than mystical ones.
Two co-defining complementary principles — bright/dry/male and dark/wet/female — whose union is the whole; a structural parallel to the Dao's paired powers.
Heaven-and-Earth as the bound creative/receptive pair — Mahatala's sky-mountain and Jata's water-deep rhyme with Qian and Kun.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Enlightenment Deists like Voltaire explicitly praised and syncretized Shangdi as the historical model of their rational, non-sectarian Supreme Being.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the underlying, harmonious, and unalterable operating principle that silently governs all existence.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both philosophies posit that the complex material universe is generated mathematically from a primary duality extending into multiplicity.
A supreme sky-source that recedes into impersonal remoteness above the working powers — the withdrawn high-god across unrelated families.

Speculative: both name an impersonal sacred order prior to any god — but aṉaṅku is dangerous voltage to be managed where the Dao is the effortless way to be aligned with. A reach of category, opposite in valence; flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are conceived as the impersonal, ever-flowing current that sustains the cosmos, which the practitioner must learn to align with and ride.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Hoasca is fundamentally conceptualized as the union of masculine force (the vine) and feminine light/receptivity (the leaf).
Both posit an impersonal right-order the cosmos announces and the wise decode — the Dao read in the cosmos, the disciplina read in liver, bolt and thunder.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The cosmos understood as an interplay of complementary male/sun/strength and female/moon/gentleness principles.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Falun emblem physically incorporates the Taiji symbols of Daoism alongside the Buddhist srivatsa to represent the whole cosmos.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Draws heavily on the Daoist concept of the supreme, unnamable way, concretizing it into three explicit moral qualities.
Two traditions render the world as a balance of cool/dark/female and hot/bright/male principles — complementarity, not warring dualism.
The supreme source who recedes from cult into impersonality — the withdrawn high-god with little direct worship.
An impersonal order the cosmos and each being obey — but Fa is a readable script of 256 signs where the Dao is the unspeakable way; a partial rhyme, flagged.
Both build a destiny-system from binary marks combined into a closed set of signs — odu and hexagram as cousins of method, no contact implied.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Yin-Yang maps the binary engine of reality (active/passive), whereas the Law of Three adds the crucial third 'neutralizing' force to allow manifestation to proceed.
GOLD SEAM: The compass circumscribing the heavens and the square measuring the earth perfectly mirror the Chinese cosmological pairing of circular Qian and square Kun.
GOLD SEAM: The ultimate truth or cosmic name which, by definition, cannot be spoken or transmitted directly, only approximated through symbols.
The mirror's hinge again: Mani makes Light and Darkness UNMIXED enemies to be sundered forever, where Yin and Yang interpenetrate and become each other — total dualism against total complementarity.
The One that cannot be named yet sources all things shares the apophatic core of the Dao that cannot be spoken — two traditions guarding the ineffable behind the world.

Both read the cosmos as a single legible correspondence rather than warring halves, but the Hermetic axiom mirrors scale (macrocosm/microcosm) while Yin-Yang mirrors polarity — two grammars of complementarity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent an underlying, non-personified cosmic matrix that flows through and constitutes all natural phenomena, explicitly rejecting external creator-deities.

Both name a right way the world is meant to follow, but loosely — the Dao a flowing impersonal order, the Great Law a deliberate human covenant modelled on the cosmos; cosmic-order shared, character opposite, flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The high, distant celestial authority who observes the world from afar and acts as the ultimate reference point in times of cosmic crisis.
Both are supreme sky-powers held in awe at a remove, worshipped less directly than the nearer gods and ancestors — high heaven kept distant by its own holiness.
An impersonal, all-pervading order that is kept in relation rather than worshipped — Sila as breath-and-rightness rhymes with the Dao as the way the cosmos moves.
Both crown non-action: ahiṃsā upholds the world by the knife withheld as the Dao's wu-wei upholds it by the deed unforced — right order kept by restraint rather than assertion.

Both refuse the single absolute claim — Yin-Yang by pairing every truth with its complement, anekāntavāda by multiplying standpoints to infinity; complementarity as two poles versus complementarity as countless.
GOLD SEAM: The nameless, ineffable root of all things that acts by not acting, prior to the dualities of creation.
GOLD SEAM: The conceptualization of the cosmos as requiring the harmonious balancing of distinct masculine and feminine principles.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The unnameable, intuitive inner current that serves as both the fabric of the universe and the method to understand it.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A comprehensive bipartite cosmology that maps fundamental elemental oppositions directly onto social and practical life.
Both supreme sky-powers held in awe at a remove while nearer spirits and ancestors receive the worship — high heaven distant by its own holiness.

Male-and-female completeness as the ground of being — but where Yin-Yang holds the two in eternal balance, Mahungu is a wholeness SPLIT, complementarity remembered as a fall. The divergence sharpens the shared joint.

A supreme Lord of Heaven who grants the warrant to rule below — Hwanin's mandate to descend rhymes with Tian/Shangdi's Mandate of Heaven; long Sinitic cultural contact makes some influence likely, but the high-sky parallel is structural.
Both a guiding cosmic-order ideal underlying right action; hongik ingan diverges by being explicitly humane and purposive — the order named as benefit-to-all rather than the impersonal Way.

Heaven's warrant cast as moral purpose: hongik ingan rhymes with the ethical conditioning of the Mandate of Heaven — the sky's sanction tied to benevolent rule; Sinitic contact makes the rhyme partly historical.
The unnameable holy whole that is in and through everything and cannot be reduced to a god — the Great Mystery and the Dao alike name reality's sacred ground rather than a being within it.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Finding the absolute and the divine not in ascetic withdrawal, but in the natural flow of daily, unforced physical action.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A transcendent celestial orchestrator who operates far removed from direct human petition.
GOLD SEAM: In the Chinese Manichaean text the Compendium of the Teaching of Mani, the Father is syncretized as Shangdi, the Venerable Light.
Both are complementary polarities — Cautes and Cautopates are raised/lowered, dawn/dusk, while yin-yang is dark/light, passive/active. A loose structural echo of complementarity, flagged.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both function as an impersonal, omnipresent, un-namable fundamental law of nature that spontaneously harmonizes all existence when a practitioner aligns with it effortlessly.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Osho's concept mirrors the Daoist integration of opposites, positing that a complete human must perfectly balance materialistic celebration and silent meditation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are supreme, impersonal celestial sovereigns who grant the mandate to rule to earthly emperors.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both conceptualize reality through an amoral, complementary dualism where opposing forces (kinetic/violent vs. still/peaceful) must be balanced to sustain cosmic order.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are conceptualized as 'The Way', an all-encompassing path of right action that aligns human behavior with cosmic harmony.

Both are the all-embracing way of harmony in which one walks rather than a god to be obeyed — hózhó the path of beauty, the Dao the path of nature; cosmic order as a way of life, separately arrived at.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The unnameable, transcendent source that effortlessly generates the manifold world without willful action or depletion.
GOLD SEAM: A fundamental, flowing substrate of reality, though the New Age attempts to harness it for personal manifestation rather than yielding to it.
GOLD SEAM: Both represent the underlying, flowing substrate of reality, though New Thought attempts to aggressively direct it rather than yield to it.
GOLD SEAM: Supreme sky sovereigns who communicate through divination and issue absolute, revokable mandates to legitimize rulers.
Both name an impersonal order the world is held in — but the potlatch's law is the law of the gift and reciprocity, not the Dao's effortless way. A reach across very different registers.
Reality organised as two complementary halves that require each other — but here it is kinship law (Raven/Eagle moieties must intermarry), not metaphysical polarity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the uncaused, spontaneous generative force that effortlessly produces the myriad things.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Discordian concept of Aneristic (order) and Eristic (chaos) illusions directly mirrors the dualistic interplay of Yin and Yang, though privileging chaos as the base reality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An ineffable state of natural flow and effortless action (wu wei), framed here through the lens of dodging wage-labor and societal expectation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the ultimate path of yielding, non-resistance, and finding harmony by simply rolling with the cosmic gutters.

Cosmos as an irreducible Heaven-and-Earth polarity emerging from a prior undifferentiation; Qián/Kūn name the primal pair as Te Kore and Te Pō ripen toward Rangi and Papa — a structural rhyme of differentiation, no contact.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The generation of all reality through the tension and balance of a primordial binary of light/dark, male/female, odd/even.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The silent, perfect, underlying frequency and rhythm that governs the movements of heaven and earth.
GOLD SEAM: Supreme emblems fusing fundamental opposites—the rigid, agonizing structure of matter and the unfolding, vital essence of the spirit.

A stretch: the two unequal San gods form a gradient of giving and troubling power, faintly like a complementarity — but it is asymmetric and unsystematised, NOT a balanced dyad. Flagged precisely to mark the divergence: the San decline a clean dualism.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are visual syntheses of the universe's binary forces—male/female, light/dark—unified into a single, functional whole.
GOLD SEAM: Both are supreme symbols containing the union of opposites, though Baphomet anchors this balance entirely within the material, carnal sphere.
GOLD SEAM: Daoism posits harmony and balance within the material universe; Scientology views the physical universe purely as a degradation to be escaped or dominated by spirit.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The dynamic polarity essential for cosmic manifestation, recognizing that stillness and action require one another to generate reality.
GOLD SEAM: The underlying flow of the universe; spiritual liberation is found not in fighting it or forcing it, but in effortless alignment with its current.
GOLD SEAM: The inextricable unity and necessary balance of the spiritual/internal and the temporal/political realms.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The immanent, natural law that silently orders the cosmos without anthropomorphic deliberation, to which human beings must align their nature ('kata physin').
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The spontaneous splitting of a primordial cosmic whole into an active light principle and a receptive dark principle, required to generate the universe.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent a pervasive, unnameable cosmic current that flows most powerfully when the human will and intellect get out of the way.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The spiritual necessity of cosmic complementarity, where Love (feminine) and Wisdom (masculine) must inevitably unite to form a complete reality.
GOLD SEAM: In religious Taoism, the philosophical concept of the Tao is explicitly personified and deified as Taishang Laojun.
GOLD SEAM: The Jade Emperor absorbed the ancient attributes of Shangdi, becoming the ultimate celestial sovereign in later imperial dynasties.

Two East/Inner-Asian high heavens reached without Indo-European descent — impartial Sky as legitimating mandate (kut / Tianming); centuries of steppe-China contact make some mutual influence likely but the parallel itself is structural.

Kut as Heaven's mandate to rule rhymes pointedly with the Chinese Mandate of Heaven (Tianming) — the sky's withdrawable warrant for a sovereign; the steppe-China political contact makes this rhyme partly historical.
GOLD SEAM: Nuit (the encompassing circle) and Hadit (the core point) map directly onto the foundational passive and active cosmic binaries.
GOLD SEAM: Both represent an effortless, frictionless alignment with one's fundamental nature and the overarching cosmic trajectory.
GOLD SEAM: Both operate as the supreme, overseeing heavenly administrator of the earthly realm, distinct from a monotheistic creator.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are conceived as the underlying, ineffable current that naturally orders the universe.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both emphasize 'wu wei' or non-doing, positing effortless alignment as the supreme method.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An abstract, sky-associated orchestrator who guarantees moral order but is approached only through intermediaries.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: An impersonal, organic cosmology where nothing exists in isolation and the whole functions as a constantly fluctuating, self-balancing system.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The totality of all existence viewed as an active, evolving current of universal becoming.
GOLD SEAM: A fundamental, non-moral dualism mapping the universe into complementary forces—cool/ancient versus hot/kinetic.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Yiguandao explicitly identifies the classical Chinese High God as the Eternal Mother, entitling her 'Mingming Shangdi'.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Reinterprets the impersonal, ineffable Dao of Laozi as the actively loving, personified Eternal Mother.

The hinge of the whole mirror: Iran takes the paired-opposites structure and makes it WAR (good must defeat evil), where China makes it harmony (each contains and becomes the other). Dualism is complementarity refused.
Where Yin is not evil but the necessary complement of Yang, Ahriman is evil that ought not to exist — the moral asymmetry that separates dualism from balance.
Both are the impersonal right-order the cosmos obeys, but Asa is moralised into a Truth one must fight FOR, while the Dao is the order one yields TO.
Both are supreme sky-powers held in distant awe while the ancestors and lesser powers receive the actual cult — high heaven worshipped less directly than the near dead.

dossier.essence: The classical religio-philosophical mainstream of China: from Shang oracle-bone divination and Zhou worship of Shangdi/Tian, through the axial-age texts — the Yijing, the Daodejing and Zhuangzi, the Confucian classics — to organized religious Daoism (from the Celestial Masters' revelation, traditionally 142 CE) and the imperial state cult that lasted until the end of the empire. Its bearers ranged from court diviners and literati to Daoist priests and self-cultivators; its central claim is that reality follows an impersonal normative pattern — Heaven's mandate, the Dao, the alternation of yin and yang — with which human order must align.
dossier.cosmos: No creator god stands at the root: the Dao generates the one, the two, and the ten thousand things (Daodejing 42), and the world is a self-ordering process of qi articulated by the complementary polarity of yin and yang — the Yijing's sixty-four hexagrams model its permutations of change. Heaven (Tian) sanctions and revokes rulership through the Mandate (tianming), read in omens and in the people's welfare. Classical ancestral religion assumes the dead persist — the hun soul ascending, the po remaining with the body — and depend on descendants' sacrifice; religious Daoism added programs for transcendence (xian immortality) through alchemy and self-cultivation.
dossier.practice: Divination, from cracked oracle bones to yarrow-stalk consultation of the Yijing; ancestor sacrifice at household and lineage altars; the emperor's calendrical sacrifices to Heaven as the state's supreme rite; and Daoist liturgy, precepts, meditation, and internal alchemy (neidan) administered by ordained priests. Confucian ritual propriety (li) extends the cult into everyday social form. What it asks is alignment — of conduct, state, and body — with the given pattern.
dossier.canon: The Yijing (Zhou-era core with the 'Ten Wings' commentaries), the Daodejing and Zhuangzi, and the Confucian Five Classics; religious Daoism's revelations are gathered in the Daozang (Daoist Canon, 1445 Ming edition).
dossier.reflects: The ultimate as pattern rather than person — an impersonal normative order occupying the place other traditions give to a creator's will.
sources: Michael J. Puett, 'To Become a God: Cosmology, Sacrifice, and Self-Divinization in Early China' (2002)
